title: Calystegia sepium
taxonomy: Calystegia sepium is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Convolvulaceae. It belongs to the genus Calystegia, which includes a group of morning glories native to the Northern Hemisphere. Calystegia sepium is also known as Hedge Bindweed or Small Bindweed. The plant is further classified as a member of the order Solanales. It is native to Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa.
uses: Calystegia sepium, also known as hedge bindweed or wild morning glory, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential benefits. The plant's roots and leaves are used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Some studies suggest that Calystegia sepium may have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness and safety for human use. Traditionally, the plant's extracts were used to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and bronchitis.
benefits: Calystegia sepium, also known as the hedge bindweed or wild morning glory, is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine for various health benefits. It is said to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce swelling and ease pain. The plant is also believed to have antispasmodic effects, making it a potential remedy for digestive issues and muscle spasms. However, more research is needed to fully understand the extent of its health benefits and potential side effects.

constituents: Calystegia sepium contains various active constituents, including glycosides, flavonoids, and alkaloids. The glycosides present in Calystegia sepium have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The flavonoids in this plant are known to possess antioxidant and antimicrobial activities. Additionally, Calystegia sepium contains a number of cardenolides, which are responsible for its cardioactive properties. The exact composition and concentration of these active constituents can vary depending on the plant's origin and growing conditions.
parts: Calystegia sepium is a plant that has been traditionally used in herbal medicine, and its medicinal parts include the leaves and stems. preparations: Calystegia sepium, also known as hedge bindweed or wild morning glory, has been used in herbal preparations for its medicinal properties. The plant's roots and leaves are used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures. Traditionally, it has been used to treat conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin issues. The plant contains alkaloids like vincristine and vinblastine, which have been found to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. side_effects: Calystegia sepium, also known as hedge bindweed or wild morning glory, can cause side effects when ingested due to its toxic properties. The plant contains glycosides, alkaloids, and other compounds that can be harmful to humans and animals if ingested in large quantities. Symptoms of poisoning may include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. In severe cases, ingestion of the plant can lead to cardiac problems, respiratory failure, and even death. It is essential to handle the plant with caution and avoid ingesting any parts of it.
